coredata.dc:title: Enhanced interlayer coupling in twisted bilayer graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xian
Yang, Mingli
coredata.dc:description: Interaction at the interface of two layers in twist bilayer graphene (TBG) and its quantum dots (QDs) is responsible for many of their intriguing properties. Using the block-localized wavefunctions (BLW) from first-principles calculations, the interlayer interaction and its evolution with twist angle are explored to address the driving forces that govern the interlayer coupling and decoupling. The interaction energy including its electrostatic, van der Waals (vdW), polarization and charge transfer (CT) contributions varies with twist angle but in different patterns. While the interaction energy is dominated by Coulomb and vdW interaction, its variation is dominated by the CT interaction. Moreover, interlayer coupling as strong as that in the full AB-stacking Bernal structure is characterized at a small and size-dependent angle from the full AA-stacking structure. The influence of interlayer stacking, atomic arrangement at the interface and cross-layer orbital overlap on interaction energy is discussed to address the twist-induced physics in TBG QDs. These findings not only deepen our understanding of the interlayer interaction nature of TBG QDs, but also suggest new approaches of optoelectronic modulation of graphene-based materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Twist bilayer graphene
Interlayer coupling
Charge transfer
Block-localized wavefunction
First-principles calculations

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22173064 and 21773159 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structures of TA (a) and TC (b) with a twist angle of θ.

(a) The relative energy (ΔErel ) of TBG144 between the untwisted and twisted structures with different functionals and basis sets. (b) The ΔErel of TBG N (N = 72, 144, 240 and 360) at CAM-B3LYP-D3/6-311G(d) level.
Energy gap (Δε, in eV) of TBG N between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO).

The electron density difference with an isovalue of 0.0002 a.u, which is obtained by taking the electron density of two monomers at the same locations as the reference, in TBG144 at θ = 0° and 18°. The density decrease is denoted by brown, while the increase by blue.

(a) Interlayer interaction energy (Eint ) of TBG144. (b) The average interlayer interaction energy (Eint ave ) of TBGN (N = 72, 144, 240 and 360). Eint = ETBG – 2EMLG where EMLG and ETBG are the energies of the monolayer disc and its bilayer complex, respectively. Computations are at the CAM-B3LYP-D3/6-311G(d) level.
Equilibrium distances of TBG N at a varying twist angle.
The interlayer interaction energy (Eint ) of TBG72 (a), TBG144 (b), TBG240 (c) and TBG360 (d) at their equilibrium distances.

(a) The total and its decomposed interaction energy of EFrozen
, ECT and EPol of TBG144 versus twist angle. (b) The corresponding η values (see text for its definition) of EFrozen
, ECT and EPol . (c) The corresponding κ values (see text for its definition) of EFrozen
, ECT and EPol .

head.ce:title: Enhanced interlayer coupling in twisted bilayer graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Key Laboratory of High Energy Density Physics of Ministry of Education, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Atomic and Molecular Physics
Key Laboratory of High Energy Density Physics of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Key Laboratory of High Energy Density Physics of Ministry of Education, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Materials Genome Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Materials Genome Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Materials Genome Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enhanced interlayer coupling occurs not only in the previously well-addressed AB stacking (Bernal structure), but also in the quasi-AA-stacking structures with a small and size-dependent twist angle. Although the interlayer interaction energy is dominated by Coulomb and van der Waals interaction, its twist-induced variation is mostly driven by the charge-transfer interaction.

Interaction at the interface of two layers in twist bilayer graphene (TBG) and its quantum dots (QDs) is responsible for many of their intriguing properties. Using the block-localized wavefunctions (BLW) from first-principles calculations, the interlayer interaction and its evolution with twist angle are explored to address the driving forces that govern the interlayer coupling and decoupling. The interaction energy including its electrostatic, van der Waals (vdW), polarization and charge transfer (CT) contributions varies with twist angle but in different patterns. While the interaction energy is dominated by Coulomb and vdW interaction, its variation is dominated by the CT interaction. Moreover, interlayer coupling as strong as that in the full AB-stacking Bernal structure is characterized at a small and size-dependent angle from the full AA-stacking structure. The influence of interlayer stacking, atomic arrangement at the interface and cross-layer orbital overlap on interaction energy is discussed to address the twist-induced physics in TBG QDs. These findings not only deepen our understanding of the interlayer interaction nature of TBG QDs, but also suggest new approaches of optoelectronic modulation of graphene-based materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Interlayer relative rotation provides an effective path for tuning the interlayer electronic coupling of van der Waals (vdW) structures [1–6]. When a small twist rotation is imposed on a bilayer graphene complex to form twisted bilayer graphene (TBG), its properties change profoundly, leading to intriguing phenomena such as superconductivity, correlated insulators and abnormal conductivity [7–9] etc. These properties are relevant to the strong interlayer electronic coupling [10–12] that occurs at a small twist angle. Interlayer rotation that results in various stacking patterns, namely moiré superlattice, is effective to affect the coupling strength and in consequence to determine the exotic physicochemical properties. When infinite TBG is reduced dimensionally into a quantum dot (QD), moreover, it exhibits properties distinct from those in bulk because of quantum confinement [13–17].

Many works have been devoted to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and their applications [18–20], and particular attention has been paid to their twisted structures [21–25]. Because of their weak spin–orbit coupling, suppressed hyperfine interaction and long spin coherence times, the single and double GQDs are well-suited for the quantum information processing as an excellent candidate for the implementation of spin qubits [18,20,26–28]. Moreover, complex interlayer decoupling and coupling were observed in the stacked graphene flakes, which are remarkably different from those in the structures with a full AA- or AB-stacking [22]. A number of studies [29–31] have been conducted to address the interlayer interacting modes and interaction energy of stacked polyaromatic hydrocarbons (PAH), which are similar with the TBG QDs in structure. Controlling over the twist angle and consequently the interlayer coupling is one of the effective tools for the manipulation of the electronic and optical properties of TBG QDs.

It has been well addressed that the decoupling occurs in the full-AA stacking with a twist angle θ = 0°, while strong coupling in the full-AB stacking, namely Bernal structure. Upon interlayer twist, the equivalent and symmetric two discs become inequivalent because of interlayer exchange, resulting in a transformation from a vdW homostructure into a vdW heterostructure. In these structures, interlayer coupling is definitely dominated by nonbonded interaction, such as vdW and Coulomb interactions. Changes in the interlayer coupling strength are reflected explicitly by the interaction energy between the two layers. Understanding to the interaction energy and its variations with twist angle is crucial in the exploration for the physical origin of many novel phenomena characterized in TBG, and in the design of various graphene-based optoelectronic devices.

A variety of energy decomposition schemes have been proposed [32–36] to distinguish the individual contributions to the interaction among different fragments, among which the block-localized wavefunction (BLW) method [37–39] combines the advantages of both molecular orbital and valence bond theories, and investigates the nature of intermolecular interactions in the light of several physically intuitive energy components. The BLW-based energy decomposition analysis (EDA) method has been successfully used to elucidate the interaction physical sources of hydrogen-bonding system, Lewis-acid pairs and vdW complexes, etc. [37–39]. In this work, we apply the BLW-EDA approach to explore how the interlayer interaction energy in TBG QDs varies with twist angle, and to distinguish its individual contributions that govern the interlayer interaction and its variations. While the vdW and Coulomb contribution is dominant in energy, as presented below, the charge transfer (CT) contribution is dominant in energy variations. Moreover, strong interlayer coupling is addressed in the structures misorientating from the full-AA stacking by a small and size-dependent angle. Based on the BLW-EDA results, the dependence of interlayer coupling in TBG QDs on the interlayer stacking, interlayer distance, disc size, and orbital overlap between the two layers is discussed for the modulation and design of the QDs with desirable interlayer interaction strength.

The TBG QDs structures are constructed based on monolayer carbon discs with diameters of 0.9 ∼ 2.4 nm among which the smallest-size disc was previously synthesized via a top-down and bottom-up method [19]. The monolayers have M (M = 2–5) shells with 6 M 2 carbon atoms and 6 M hydrogen atoms, respectively. The four studied QDs are then referred to as TBG N (N = 12 M 2 + 12 M = 72, 144, 240 and 360). The interlayer twist occurs by rotating the upper layer at an angle of θ and fixing the bottom layer. A rigid flat structure is assumed for each layer during the interlayer rotation. It is a reasonable assumption since the opposite layers in experiments are adhered respectively to their substrates rather than in the gaseous phase, which makes their structures rigid while tuning the twist angle. The substrate effect is not considered in this work. The rotation axis has two options. One passes through the carbon atom of the innermost shell, denoted as TA, as displayed in the Fig. 1 (a). For TA, the full-AB (Bernal) stacking appears at θ = 60°. The other passes through the center of the innermost shell, denoted as TC, as shown in Fig. 1(b). TC has a period of θ = 30°. Both TA and TC have a full-AA stacking at θ = 0°, in which one atom in one layer is right above one atom in the opposite layer. For the other angles, the structures are the intermediate stackings between AA and AB stackings, which are referred to as SP hereafter. Since the TA and TC models exhibit similar variations with the twist angle, the computed properties of Model TA are mainly discussed, followed by a brief comparison with the properties of Model TC.

The interlayer distance (DL) is crucial for the interlayer coupling stength, which is set to 3.34 Å, an interlayer separation in many studies [22,40–43]. A biconcave distortion was predicted for the AA and AB structures of polyaromatic hydrocarbons (PAH) using high-level ab initio calculations [29]. In addition to the calculations on the structures with a fixed DL = 3.34 Å, the DL dependence of interlayer polarizability is thus studied for the rigid TBG144 structures [44].

In the BLW-EDA analysis scheme, an initial electron-localized state (BLW0), an optimal electron-localized state (BLW) and a final electron-delocalized state (standard DFT) are defined, respectively. [37,38] The total electrons and basis functions are decomposed accordingly into several blocks that correspond respectively to different fragments in the interacting system. [37,38] For a TBG QD, it is decomposed into two monomers, the upper and the lower layers. Its total energy of optimal electron-localized state is represented with E[ϕ BLW ] in which ϕ BLW is the relaxed block-localized molecular wave function for the whole system, while E[ϕ BLW0 ] is the initial energy obtained using the molecular wave function ϕ BLW0 constructed from isolated monomers.

Interaction energy, Eint , is decomposed into three terms of.
(1)
E
int
=
E
Frozen
+
E
Pol
+
E
CT
where
E
Frozen
, EPol, and
E
CT
are the sum of electrostatic and van der Waals energies, the polarization energy and the charge transfer energy, respectively. EFrozen is calculated with E[ϕ BLW0 ], and monomer energies (Eup and Elow ). In order to properly describe the dispersion interaction in the DFT calculations, a dispersion correction (ΔEdis ) is always added into EFrozen
[37]. One then has
(2)
E
Frozen
= E
ϕ
B L W 0
-
E
up
-
E
low
+
Δ E
dis

E
Pol
represents the energy variation due to the response of block-localized molecular wave functions against the electric field induced by other monomers, given by.
(3)
E
Pol
= E
[
ϕ
BLW
]
- E
[
ϕ
B L W 0
]

Furthermore, the electron delocalizes from block-localized orbitals into the whole TBG QD (EQD ) to stabilize the system. The basis set superposition errors (BSSE) correction, which is evaluated with the counterpoise method [45], should be added into the charge transfer energy term [37], which is defined as.
(4)
E
CT
=
E
QD
- E
[
ϕ
BLW
]
+ B S S E

The optimization of monolayer and the interaction energy of TBG QDs are computed with the modified GAMESS package [46]. Both dispersion correction and the diffuse functions are imperative for the description of vdW structures. The exchange–correlation functionals, M06-2X [47], CAM-B3LYP [48] and ωB97XD [49], including long-range and vdW corrections, and the standard 6–31++G(d,p) and 6-311G(d) basis sets [50] are used in the calculations. The Grimme's 2010 dispersion (D3) correction is employed with the DFT-D3 V3.01 package [51]. These settings have produced reliable interaction energy for many vdW systems [52–55].

The energy of TBG QDs varies upon interlayer twist. Taking untwisted structure as a reference, Fig. 2 (a) presents the relative energy (ΔErel ) of TBG144, which is the difference between the untwisted and twisted structures. The interlayer distance is fixed to 3.34 Å [22,40–43] in the calculations. The full-AA stacking structure at θ = 0° has the highest energy. With increasing θ, its energy decreases rapidly, bottoms at θ = 18°, and oscillates until θ = 60° at which the lowest-energy structure is identified. All the six combinations of CAM-B3LYP and ωB97XD functionals, 6-31G(d,p), 6–31++G(d,p) and 6-311G(d) basis sets, produce similar ΔErel variations. The computed energy landscapes are in good agreement with previous study [22]. The Bernal structure (θ = 60°) is the most stable stacking in bilayer graphene and graphite [2,56–58]. However, some twisted structures, in particular to the one at θ = 18°, have comparable stability with the Bernal structure. The Bernal structure of TBG144 is only 7.3 meV/atom lower than the full-AA structure at the CAM-B3LYP/6-311G(d) level.

The relative stability varies in a similar way for the larger discs, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The ΔErel values of the four sizes peak at θ = 0°, bottom at θ = 60°, and oscillate at the intermediate angles. However, two differences are noted. First, the first local minima (θf ) are around 29°, 18°, 13° and 10° for the four sizes, respectively, demonstrating a clear trend that the local minima occur at a smaller angle for larger systems. At θf , the ΔErel values are about −5.5, −6.0, −6.1 and −6.2 meV/atom, respectively, indicating a deeper well for larger-sized discs. Second, the number of local minima and maxima increases with the disc size. TBG72 has one peak in (0°, 60°), while the numbers of peaks are 2, 3 and 4 for the larger systems, respectively. The periodic variations in the energetics and properties of twisted multilayer graphene have been noted in some other studies [2,9,44,59]. Both differences are attributed to the occurrence of AB stacking in different shells. Departing from the full AA stacking, the AB stacking first appears between atoms in the outermost shell, followed by the secondary outer and inner shells. The θf values correspond to the occurrence of quasi-AB stacking in the outermost shell. It is apparent that the AB stacking appears at a smaller twist angle for larger-sized discs. Moreover, the AB stacking appears more frequent in the larger-sized systems during interlayer twist.

The interlayer twist leads to some changes in the electronic structures of TBG N . Fig. 3 presents the changes in the energy gap (Δε) TBG N . Taking TBG144 as an example, Δε increases rapidly at small θ, but the increase becomes slow after θ = 18°. Δε reaches its maximum at about 40°. After some decrease, it increases again until 60°. The structures of other three sizes have the similar trend except some shifts in Δε. The Δε decreases with the QD size, stemming from decreasing quantum confinement in large conjugation systems.

Furthermore, the surface distributions of the highest-occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest-unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), electrostatic potential surface (EPS), the electron density change are compared for the untwisted and the twisted (θ = 18° at which the first peak of ΔErel occurs) TBG144 structures, as presented in Fig. 4 and Figures S1 and S2 in Supplementary Material (SM). All the HOMOs and LUMOs (Figure S1) are dominated by the 2pz of carbon atoms. While their LUMOs exhibit strong interlayer overlap, their HOMOs are quite different for the two structures. In the untwisted structure, the opposite atoms are face-to-face and many of them have their 2pz orbitals opposite in sign in the interlayer region, disfavoring their interlayer overlap. In the twisted structure, more atoms, especially those at the outermost shell, have their 2pz orbitals matchable for interlayer overlap, which tends to enhance the interlayer interaction. The EPS contours (Figure S2) do not show a remarkable change upon interlayer twist in the interlayer region from the side view, but from the top view the potentials around the carbon atoms become more positive, while those in outside region along the atoms in outermost shell become more negative, indicating a stronger intralayer electrostatic potential in the twisted structure. Fig. 4 presents the electron density change after two monomers form an untwisted/twisted TBG. A remarkable density reduction in the interlayer region is noted in the untwisted, while in the structure of θ = 18° the density from two monolayers remains almost unchanged because of their interlayer overlap.

It is evident that the electronic properties of TBGN depends strongly on the twist angle, which alters the relative locations of carbon atoms between the two layers. All carbon atoms are in an AA stacking pattern in which every atom in the top layer is right above an atom in the bottom layer at θ = 0°. All atoms in one layer except the central one on the rotation axis are displaced relative to the atoms in the other layer when the rotation starts.

The ΔErel variations often reflect the changes in the interlayer coupling strength of TBG QDs. Fig. 5 (a) presents their corresponding interaction energy (Eint ) between the two discs of TBG144. For all the four sizes (Fig. 5b), their largest Eint are found at θ = 60°, followed by θ = θf , while the smallest Eint are at θ = 0°. While the relatively strong interlayer coupling in the Bernal (full-AB stacking) structure has been well addressed, our calculations reveal that strong coupling may exist in the quasi-AA stacking structures at θ = θf .

In addition, the influence of interlayer distance (dint ) on Eint is investigated, as shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 . dint is optimized while interlayer twist. The dint variations are similar for the four structures. For example, the equilibrium distance of TBG144 is about 3.50 Å at 0°, decreases with θ, and keeps almost constant (about 3.34 Å) when θ = 14–43°, then further decreases to about 3.23 Å at 60° (Fig. 6). The shorter dint at 60° compared to the measured distance (3.35 Å) in bulk results from the missing of attraction forces in the bilayer structures from the outer shells. A closer distance indicates a stronger interaction, which verifies the Eint variations as mentioned above. Furthermore, if the Eint is computed with the structures at their equilibrium interlayer distances (Fig. 7), their Eint varies in the similar way with those presented in Fig. 5. It should be mentioned that the magnitude of Eint reflects the behaviors of all atoms in the TBGN structures. Both their interlayer stacking patterns and their numbers in each stacking make influence on Eint . In addition, the intra-layer interatomic interaction also changes to some extent with the twist angle. Therefore, the local minima with the largest Eint cannot be characterized solely on the occurrence of AB-stacking, although the atoms in AB-stacking usually have greater contribution to Eint than the atoms in AA-stacking.

The BLW-EDA method is then applied to the Eint of TBG144 at a varying twist angle. Eint is decomposed into EFrozen , ECT and EPol , as displayed in Fig. 8 (a). The Eint is dominated by EFrozen at all angles, reflecting the fact that the TBG is essentially a vdW junction in which electrostatic and vdW forces contribute most to its interlayer interaction, as addressed in many other vdW complexes [37,38,51]. The second contribution is from ECT , while EPol is ignorable. Both EFrozen and ECT vary with θ, maximizing at 0° and decreasing with θ. Specifically, EFrozen decreases from −14.70 to –32.45 kcal/mol, and ECT decreases from −2.69 to −8.94 kcal/mol, which are changed by 17.75 and 6.25 kcal/mol, respectively. Considering the corresponding total Eint variations from −18.5 to −42.7 kcal/mol, one may note that the proportion from EFrozen decreases, while that from ECT increases upon interlayer twist. The contribution, ηx , of the three components, is defined as.
(5)
η x
=
E x
E
int
× 100 % , x = F r o z e n ,
C T
a n d
P o l

The computed ηx is depicted in Fig. 8(b). ηCT increases from about 15% at 0° to about 25% at 18° where the first peak stands. The corresponding ηFrozen decreases from about 80% to 72%, and ηPol from about 8% to 2%. In light of interaction energy, the weighting of interlayer charge transfer term increases during interlayer twist, while those of frozen and polarization terms decrease. The proportions of these three terms vary with interlayer distances. Table 1S in SM lists the proportions of the corresponding stationary structures at θ = 0 and 60° in which the interlayer distances are relaxed. It is apparent that the proportions of the charge transfer and polarization terms increase with the interlayer distance, while that of the frozen term decreases.

By taking the corresponding items of the untwisted structure as a reference, the Eint variations caused by interlayer twist can be further highlighted. One defines.
(6)
κ x
=
E x
-
E
0 , x
E
0 , x
where Ex is the EFrozen , ECT and EPol components, respectively, and E0,x is the corresponding item of the untwisted structure. κx measures the deviation of Ex from E0,x . As depicted in Fig. 8(c), ECT has the largest κ, indicating its high sensitivity to the twist angle. κCT increases quickly from 0°, reaches its first peak at about 18°, then oscillates until maximizing at 60°. κCT reaches about 2.0 at 18° and about 2.3 at 60°, which means a remarkable change compared to κFrozen and κPol . The corresponding κFrozen and κPol are within 1.2 and 0.2, respectively. Moreover, the κCT variation has the same oscillations with those of the total Ex, implying an important finding that the twist-induced variations in interlayer coupling are mainly determined by the charge transfer contributions.

Similar dependence of ΔErel and Eint on the twist angle is noted for the TC structures with a different rotation axis, as presented in Figures. S3 and S4. Rather small differences can be found between the TA and TC structures. It is expected that the discrepancies resulting from the rotation axes become ignorable when the QD size becomes large.

All carbon atoms in graphene are in sp 2 hybridization, forming a conjugation cycle in its six-membered ring and in the whole monolayer disc. Every carbon atom has one valence electron in its 2pz orbital, which is perpendicular to the conjugation plane. The six-membered conjugation cycle is characterized by its electrophilic activity at the hollow site. When the two layers are stacked in TBG, both the 2pz and the conjugation path in one layer exert influence on the 2pz and the conjugation path in the opposite layer, forming various interlayer interactions, namely pz –pz , pz –π and π–π [60,61]. pz –pz occurs between carbon atoms just above another, pz –π between one carbon atom above the six-membered ring, and π–π between two stacking rings.

Since the hollow site of the six-membered carbon rings is negatively charged, the π–π interaction runs as a Coulomb repulsion. The C-2pz orbitals can be phase matched or mismatched between the two layers, the interlayer pz –pz overlap is allowed or forbidden depending on the stacking. In contrast, the pz –π attraction favors the interlayer coupling and tends to stabilize the bilayer system. However, pz –π attraction mostly occurs in the AB stacking where pz is above the hollow site of six-membered ring. The full-AA stacking at 0° has the weakest pz –π attraction, but strongest π–π repulsion. The C-2pz orbitals are also mostly phase mismatched with opposite signs [59]. Therefore, the weakest interlayer coupling is found at 0°. Upon the interlayer twist, the relative displacements of carbon atoms weaken the π–π repulsion, changing some mismatched pz–pz interactions into matched ones. Moreover, the formation of AB-stacking leads to pz –π attraction. As a result, the interlayer coupling is strengthened once the interlayer twist departs from the full-AA stacking. The AB stacking first appears at the outermost shell, and then the inner shells one by one, resulting in the periodically strengthened coupling. The full-AB stacking at 60° has the strongest pz –π attraction and the weakest π–π repulsion. Many studies [2,56–58] have addressed the strong coupling in the Bernal stacking multilayer graphene. Our calculations reveal that the strong coupling occurs not only in the Bernal structure (θ = 60°), but also in the structures rotating from the full-AA structure by a small angle.

The vdW and Coulomb contributions in the EFrozen term are dependent on the interatomic distances between the two layers. The structure at 0° has the shortest interatomic distances since all carbon atoms in one layer are just above the corresponding carbon atoms in the other layer. The vdW interaction is dominated by attraction forces at the interlayer C–C distances of 3.34 Å or longer, while the Coulomb interaction is repulsive because the atoms in the opposite sites usually possess similar charge with each other. Upon interlayer twist, the increasing C–C distances lead to weaker vdW attraction and weaker Coulomb repulsion. The full-AB structures at θ = 60° have the longest C–C distances with the weakest Coulomb repulsion. Meanwhile, the pz –π Coulomb attraction reaches its strongest point. Therefore, the twist dependence of EFrozen is caused by the changes of interlayer C–C distances and the interplay among the pz –pz , pz –π and π–π interaction.

During the interlayer twist of θ = 0–60° in TBG144, the variations in atomic net charge (Hirshfeld analysis [62]) are rather small (less than 0.001 a.u.), and so are the corresponding variations in atomic dipole moments. Such small differences in atomic net charge and dipole moment have rather limited influence on EPol , which exhibits small changes with twist angle. In our previous study [44] the polarizability of TBG QDs was studied using a polarizability decomposition scheme in which the charge transfer contribution plays a vital role in interlayer polarizability.

ECT is mainly contributed by the overlap of the Kohn-Sham orbitals between the two discs. As analyzed above, the repulsive π–π interaction and phase mismatched pz –pz interaction disfavor the interlayer overlap, resulting in small ECT contribution to Eint in the full-AA structure. The increasing phase matched pz –pz interaction upon interlayer twist leads to promoted interlayer overlap and enhanced ECT . Particularly, the occurrence of pz –π interaction when AB-stacking first appears at θ = θf generates great interlayer overlap and the first ECT peak. The alternate appearance of AB stacking in the different shells keeps the enhanced ECT until it reaches its maximum at 60°. Therefore, the ECT variations are caused by the fluctuations of pz –pz and pz –π overlap during interlayer twist. Considering that the pz –π overlap does not exist in the full-AA structure, its occurrence upon interlayer twist exhibits great influence on Eint . In contrast, the vdW attraction and Coulomb interactions vary inversely with the sixth and second powers of interatomic distances, respectively, making them less sensitive to the interlayer twist than the pz –pz and pz –π overlap when the interlayer distance is fixed to about 3.34 Å.

The Eint changes resulting from interlayer twist definitely lead to some changes in the electronic properties. The HOMO-LUMO gap, Δε, becomes wide for the structure with a large Eint in absolute value, so are the great amount in interlayer charge transfer, the enhanced interlayer overlap in their HOMO and LUMO orbitals and strong electrostatic potential in the intralayer region. In fact, these quantities vary in the same pattern, each of which has influence on the others. One notes the direct correlation of Eint with these quantities. As Eint is dominated by EFrozen , and its variation with the twist angle is mainly determined by ECT , the twist-induced property variations are thus mainly dependent on ECT . The ECT term is described by the wavefunction overlap between the adjacent layers and sensitive to the twist angle and external fields in the direction perpendicular to the graphene layers [6,44,59], suggesting that the application of interlayer twist and external fields can be used to modulate the electronic properties of TBG QDs.

Using a bilayer graphene quantum dot model, the interlayer coupling is investigated by the DFT-based BLW-EDA method in which the interlayer interaction energy is decomposed into Coulomb, vdW, polarization and CT contributions, allowing us to track their variations with twist angle. The interaction energy bottoms at θ = 0°, increases with θ, reaches its first peak at θf , and fluctuates until it maximizes at θ = 60°. θf is 29°, 18°, 13° and 10° for the four QDs, decreasing with the QD size. For TBG144, EFrozen , ECT and EPol account for about 75–80%, 15–23% and 3–5% in the total Eint , respectively, all of which are enhanced by interlayer twist.

However, the ECT contribution remarkably increases with twist angle, indicating its key role in the twist-induced Eint variations. The energy evolution is related to the stacking changes caused by interlayer twist. The Eint maxima usually occur at the angles when the AB stacking appears. Particularly, the first peak in Eint corresponds to the first occurrence of AB-stacking in the outermost shell of the QDs, indicating that strong interlayer coupling occurs not only in the full-AB stacking Bernal structures, but also in the structures slightly mis-orientating from the AA stacking. The interplay among the pz –pz , pz –π and π–π interactions varies with the twist angle, resulting in the enhanced interlayer coupling in the twisted structures. While the vdW and Coulomb interactions vary with interatomic distances, the CT is dependent on the cross-layer overlap of Kohn-Sham orbitals, resulting in their different sensitivities to twist angle. Our study addresses the physical origin of the twist-induced interlayer coupling variations, which would be useful for the manipulation of electronic structures of TBG QDs.
Xian Wang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mingli Yang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22173064 and 21773159).
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.154148.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
